overview: This week I converted the Feature Spec Generator from a CLI script into a Streamlit web app, deployed it to Streamlit Cloud with a shareable URL, and wired up Microsoft Teams webhook notifications for visibility. The five-day sprint ended with a live rollout to my PM team, generating 12 completed specs on Day 5 alone and surfacing a backlog of real user feedback that's already shaping v3.

keyPoints.title: App went live on Streamlit Cloud in under 10 minutes
keyPoints.description: Once I connected my GitHub repo and configured secrets in the Streamlit Cloud dashboard, the app deployed with a public URL faster than any previous week's infrastructure work.

keyPoints.title: Teams webhook notification took only 10 minutes to set up
keyPoints.description: I wired the app to post a notification card to a standard Teams channel on every spec generation, giving the team instant, zero-dashboard visibility into real usage.

keyPoints.title: 12 completed specs generated by PMs on Day 5 rollout
keyPoints.description: After walking the team through the tool and sharing a feedback form, the Teams channel logged 12 spec-generation notifications by end of day, plus a wave of actionable feature requests.

keyPoints.title: Claude silently shipped v1 instead of the v2 multi-agent build
keyPoints.description: I didn't discover Claude had reverted to the Week 2 single-agent version until I ran the app locally, but the cheaper, simpler build proved to be the right call for capturing real-world feedback over raw capability.

keyPoints.title: Five Streamlit concepts unlocked the entire multi-stage app flow
keyPoints.description: Session state, conditional rendering, st.rerun(), secrets management, and spinners for 30–60 second API calls were the core primitives that made the Input → Q&A → Generate → Result flow work in a single file.

keyPoints.title: Private Teams channels block webhook connector support
keyPoints.description: I discovered that connector support is restricted in private channels, so I used a standard channel instead and the webhook worked immediately with no additional configuration.

keyPoints.title: Week 5's hardest work was operational, not technical
keyPoints.description: Secrets management, repo structure, notification wiring, and writing the team announcement required more deliberate effort than the Streamlit conversion or deployment itself.
